title: Cat Walking in Circles to the Left: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is walking in circles to the left, when to see a vet, and how to identify signs of vestibular disease or neurological issues.
content_html:
What is Circling Behavior?

When a cat begins walking in persistent circles to one side, it typically indicates a disruption in their vestibular system. This system, which involves the inner ear and specific parts of the brain, is responsible for maintaining balance and orientation. When one side is affected, the cat feels as though they are tilting or falling, leading them to circle toward the side of the lesion.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is classified as Medium. While walking in circles is rarely an immediate life-threatening event like a respiratory arrest, it is a sign of a significant neurological or middle-ear problem. You should contact your veterinarian or an urgent care clinic to schedule an appointment within the next 24 hours. Immediate emergency care is recommended if the circling is accompanied by seizures, loss of consciousness, or extreme lethargy.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a video of your cat circling is one of the most helpful tools you can provide to a veterinary team. Cats often experience stress at the clinic which can temporarily mask or alter their behavioral symptoms. A clear video allows the veterinarian to observe the exact diameter of the circle, the presence of a head tilt, and any rhythmic eye movements (nystagmus) that help differentiate between an ear infection and a brain-related issue.

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause my cat to walk in circles?
faqs.answer: Yes, a severe infection in the middle or inner ear is one of the most common reasons for vestibular signs like circling and head tilting.

faqs.question: Is my cat dizzy when they circle?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats walking in circles are often experiencing a sensation similar to vertigo, which can also cause nausea and a decreased appetite.

faqs.question: Could circling be a sign of a stroke?
faqs.answer: While less common in cats than in humans, vascular events or 'strokes' in the brain can cause sudden neurological changes, including circling to one side.
